threats.title: Public _addCredIdPerAddress/_removeCredIdPerAddress Enables Permanent Share Sell DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: _addCredIdPerAddress and _removeCredIdPerAddress are declared public with no access control. An attacker can call _removeCredIdPerAddress(credId, victim) to corrupt the victim's cred tracking array. When the victim later tries to sell their last share, _updateCuratorShareBalance calls _removeCredIdPerAddress internally, but the index mapping is now stale/deleted, causing a WrongCredId revert. The victim's shares and underlying bonding curve ETH are permanently locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code-level access control defect—two functions named with underscore (suggesting internal) are marked public. The direct economic impact is permanent fund locking for any targeted user holding cred shares.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function _addCredIdPerAddress(uint256 credId_, address sender_) public and function _removeCredIdPerAddress(uint256 credId_, address sender_) public — no access control modifiers

preconditions: Target victim holds shares for a credId
Attacker calls _removeCredIdPerAddress(credId, victim) before victim sells

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: External caller corrupts tracking indices; subsequent internal _removeCredIdPerAddress call during sell finds stale/wrong index → WrongCredId revert
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker locks victim's shares permanently; could be combined with short positions or competitive griefing. Cost: only gas.

economic_questions: What is the total value at risk across all cred share holders?
Can the attack also be used via _addCredIdPerAddress to create duplicate entries causing further state corruption?
Can a victim recover by re-buying and selling to reset tracking state?
validation_requirements: Construct PoC: attacker calls _removeCredIdPerAddress(credId, victim), then victim attempts sell → verify revert
Test whether _addCredIdPerAddress with duplicate credIds creates additional corruption paths
Verify whether any code path can restore corrupted tracking state

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas cost only (~0.001 ETH on L2)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Locks victim's entire share position (potentially unbounded ETH value)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Not directly profitable, but enables competitive griefing or extortion

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after victim holds shares

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any cred share holder targeted by attacker
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All ETH locked in bonding curve for affected shares

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: No parameters needed—pure access control bug
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify WrongCredId revert path after index corruption

threats.title: Unsigned Quantity in Claim Signatures Allows Minting Up to maxSupply with Single Signature
threats.hypothesis_description: In signatureClaim, the signed encodeData_ contains (expiresIn, minter, ref, verifier, artId, chainId, data) but does NOT include the quantity or imageURI from mintArgs_. A user possessing any valid signature can modify the quantity parameter to mint up to maxSupply tokens in a single call. Similarly, merkleClaim leaf is keccak256(abi.encode(minter_, leafPart_)) which also excludes quantity. This bypasses any intended per-user quantity limits imposed by the signer.
threats.investigation_rationale: The signature verification only covers identity and art parameters, not the economic parameters (quantity). This allows any authorized minter to exhaust maxSupply, denying other eligible users and potentially extracting value if NFTs have secondary market worth.

proof_of_signal.pattern: signatureClaim decodes encodeData_ for signature verification but mintArgs_.quantity is a separate calldata parameter not covered by signature. keccak256(encodeData_) is verified but quantity_ comes from mintArgs_

preconditions: A valid signature or merkle proof exists for the minter
art.maxSupply > 1
Minter has sufficient ETH to pay mint fees for desired quantity

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Signature covers abi.encode(expiresIn_, minter_, ref_, verifier_, artId, block.chainid, data_) — no quantity field
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call signatureClaim with modified mintArgs_.quantity = maxSupply while reusing the same valid signature
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Minter captures entire NFT supply, potentially reselling on secondary markets or denying other eligible users

economic_questions: What is the secondary market value of the minted NFTs?
Can the same signature be reused multiple times if quantity < maxSupply per call?
Does artMinted tracking prevent repeat claims?
validation_requirements: Confirm artMinted is set but never checked as a precondition
Construct PoC: obtain valid signature for quantity=1, call signatureClaim with quantity=maxSupply
Verify merkleClaim has the same issue with quantity not in leaf

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: quantity * (artistReward + mintFee + referralReward + verifierReward + curateReward + mintProtocolFee)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas + mint fees for maxSupply tokens
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on NFT secondary market value and scarcity premium
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially high if NFTs have collectible value

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after obtaining valid signature, before others mint
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Exhausts maxSupply, denies all other eligible minters

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All other eligible minters for the same art

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: maxSupply for target art, mintFee rates
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify artMinted[artId_][minter_] = true is NOT checked as prerequisite

threats.title: buyShareCredFor Enables Continuous Sell-Lock Griefing on Any Curator
threats.hypothesis_description: buyShareCredFor allows anyone to buy shares credited to any curator address. This sets lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp, resetting the 10-minute sell lock period for the target curator. An attacker can repeatedly call buyShareCredFor with amount=1 for a victim's address, continuously preventing them from selling their shares. The cost is only the price of 1 share at current supply.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lock period is applied to the curator_ parameter rather than msg.sender. This creates an economic griefing vector where an attacker can lock a victim's position during unfavorable price movements, causing the victim to suffer losses from inability to exit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp — sets lock period for curator_ not for msg.sender. buyShareCredFor passes user-supplied curator_ address

preconditions: Victim holds cred shares they wish to sell
Attacker has enough ETH to buy 1 share repeatedly (bonding curve minimum price)
Current supply < MAX_SUPPLY (999)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Cost per grief = price of 1 share at current supply + protocol fee + creator fee. At low supply ~0.001 ETH per grief.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call buyShareCredFor(credId, 1, victim, 0) every ~9 minutes to keep victim permanently locked
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not directly profitable but enables: 1) Forcing losses during price drops 2) Competitive suppression 3) Extortion

economic_questions: What is the minimum cost per lock refresh at various supply levels?
Can batchBuyShareCred lock multiple creds for a victim simultaneously?
Is the attacker's share purchase itself recoverable after lock period?
validation_requirements: Calculate 1-share buy price at various supply levels
Verify batchBuyShareCred also resets lock for curator_ parameter
Test continuous lock griefing scenario over multiple blocks

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: ~0.001 ETH per lock refresh at low supply
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.001 ETH gas per transaction on L2
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect profit from forcing victim to hold during price decline
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on victim's position size and price movement

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During price decline or when victim signals intent to sell

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Unable to sell shares; losses from price decline during lock
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Victim's entire position in targeted cred

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm lock resets via buyShareCredFor with amount=1

threats.title: Curator Reward Siphoning via Strategic Share Accumulation Before distribute()
threats.hypothesis_description: CuratorRewardsDistributor.distribute() reads curator share balances at call time and distributes accumulated ETH proportionally. An attacker can: 1) Observe accumulated rewards via balanceOf[credId], 2) Buy a large number of shares to gain a dominant share percentage, 3) Call distribute() to capture the majority of rewards, 4) Wait 10 minutes and sell shares. If the reward pool is large relative to the buy/sell spread, the attack is profitable.
threats.investigation_rationale: The share lock period prevents atomic exploitation but doesn't prevent multi-block manipulation. The rewards snapshot is taken at distribute() call time with no time-weighted mechanism, creating a just-in-time acquisition incentive.

proof_of_signal.pattern: distribute reads share balances at call time via credContract.getCuratorAddresses and credContract.getShareNumber — no time-weighted averaging or snapshot mechanism

preconditions: Significant rewards accumulated in balanceOf[credId]
Attacker can buy enough shares to dominate total supply
10-minute holding period acceptable (share price risk)
Bonding curve spread + fees < rewards captured

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: userRewards = (distributeAmount * userAmounts) / totalNum — rewards proportional to current share count with no time component
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Buy N shares → call distribute() → captured = distributeAmount * N / (existingShares + N) → sell after 10 min
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Profit = capturedRewards - (buyCost - sellRevenue) - fees. Profitable when rewards pool > buy/sell spread.

economic_questions: What is the typical reward accumulation rate and pool size per credId?
What is the bonding curve spread for bulk share purchases at various supply levels?
What is the minimum reward pool for profitability at different share purchase sizes?
validation_requirements: Model bonding curve buy/sell spread for 10, 50, 100 share positions
Calculate break-even reward pool size for each position size
Simulate multi-block attack with realistic parameters

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Depends on supply level; 10 shares at supply=10 might cost ~0.01-0.1 ETH
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for buy + distribute + sell (~0.003 ETH on L2)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to ~90% of reward pool minus spread costs
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Profitable when reward pool > 2x buy/sell spread

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Capital for share purchases on bonding curve
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When balanceOf[credId] is large and existing share count is low

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Existing curators of the targeted cred
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire accumulated reward pool for the credId

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Bonding curve cost modeling at various supply points

threats.title: Excess ETH Permanently Trapped in PhiFactory via claim() Self-Call Pattern
threats.hypothesis_description: PhiFactory.claim() calculates the exact mintFee and forwards only that amount via this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee} or this.signatureClaim{value: mintFee}. Any excess ETH sent by the user above mintFee remains in the PhiFactory contract with no refund to the caller. Additionally, within _processClaim, the refund logic sends to _msgSender() which resolves to address(this) when called through the self-call path, further trapping any overpayment.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a systematic ETH trapping mechanism. Every user who sends even slightly more ETH than required loses the excess permanently (recoverable only by the owner via withdraw() to protocolFeeDestination).

proof_of_signal.pattern: claim() calls this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee}(...) — only mintFee forwarded, excess stays in contract. In _processClaim: _msgSender().safeTransferETH(etherValue_ - mintFee) sends refund to address(this)

preconditions: User calls claim() with msg.value > exact mintFee
Common scenario: user estimates fee with buffer or uses frontend that rounds up

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: this.merkleClaim{value: mintFee}(...) — excess = msg.value - mintFee stays in PhiFactory
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not a manipulation per se, but systematic value extraction from users to protocol owner
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Over time, accumulated excess could be significant; owner extracts via withdraw()

economic_questions: How frequently do users overpay mint fees?
What is the average overpayment amount?
Does batchClaim have the same issue?
validation_requirements: Call claim() with msg.value = mintFee + 0.1 ETH, verify 0.1 ETH stuck in factory
Verify _processClaim refund goes to address(this) in self-call path
Test batchClaim for same trapping behavior

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Accumulated user overpayments extractable by owner

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users calling claim() with any excess ETH

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: mintProtocolFee, artistReward, referralReward, verifierReward, curateReward, art.mintFee

threats.title: CuratorRewardsDistributor Royalty Fee Extracted by Any Caller of distribute()
threats.hypothesis_description: The distribute() function sends royaltyfee + rounding dust to msg.sender (the caller). The default withdrawRoyalty is 100 (1% of total balance). Any external account can call distribute(credId) and receive this fee. While intended as a distribution incentive, it creates a free value extraction opportunity for bots that monitor balanceOf[credId] accumulation.
threats.investigation_rationale: On chains without public mempools, bots can still monitor on-chain state (balanceOf[credId]) after each block and call distribute when profitable. The 1% royalty on potentially large accumulated balances represents meaningful extraction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _msgSender().safeTransferETH(royaltyfee + distributeAmount - actualDistributeAmount) — sends royalty fee to any caller

preconditions: balanceOf[credId] > 0
credId exists with at least one shareholder

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: royaltyfee = (totalBalance * 100) / 10000 = 1% of totalBalance, sent to caller
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Monitor balanceOf[credId] on-chain, call distribute() when profitable after accounting for gas
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If 1 ETH accumulated, caller extracts 0.01 ETH + rounding dust

economic_questions: What is the average and maximum accumulation in balanceOf[credId]?
Is the 1% fee sufficient to cover L2 gas costs?
How quickly do rewards accumulate to make calling profitable?
validation_requirements: Call distribute() from arbitrary address, verify royalty sent to caller
Calculate gas cost vs royalty for various balance levels
Monitor accumulation rates in production

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI for large accumulated balances

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: 1% fee extraction from curator reward pool

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 1% reduction in expected rewards (design intent unclear)

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify royalty calculation and ETH transfer to caller

threats.title: withdrawFor Allows Forced Withdrawal of Any User's PhiRewards Balance Without Authorization
threats.hypothesis_description: RewardControl.withdrawFor(from, amount) has no access control—any caller can trigger a withdrawal from any address's balance, sending ETH to the 'from' address. With amount=0 (FULL_BALANCE sentinel), the entire balance is force-withdrawn. While funds go to the rightful owner, this enables griefing (forcing ETH onto contracts with expensive/reverting receive functions) and disrupts composability with protocols that accumulate PhiRewards balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function's lack of authorization means any external caller can control the timing and amount of any user's reward withdrawal. This could disrupt DeFi composability and enable gas-griefing attacks on contract accounts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function withdrawFor(address from, uint256 amount) external { _withdraw(from, from, amount); } — no msg.sender check, no authorization requirement

preconditions: Target address has a non-zero balanceOf in PhiRewards
Target address can receive ETH (for griefing: target is contract with expensive receive)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: FULL_BALANCE sentinel: if amount == 0, amount = balance → full withdrawal forced
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call withdrawFor(victim, 0) to force full withdrawal to victim's address
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not directly profitable; enables griefing, forced ETH receipt on contracts, disrupts composability

economic_questions: Which contracts or users hold significant PhiRewards balances?
Are there contracts receiving rewards that would revert on forced ETH receipt?
Could forced withdrawal timing create tax or accounting issues for users?
validation_requirements: Call withdrawFor(target, 0) and verify full balance withdrawn without target's consent
Test with a contract target that has expensive receive() function
Verify no authorization checks exist

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Forced premature withdrawal; potential revert for contract accounts
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All PhiRewards balances subject to forced withdrawal

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm _withdraw sends to 'from' address when called via withdrawFor

threats.title: MerkleRoot Overwrite Invalidates Existing Merkle Claims When New Art Created for Same CredId
threats.hypothesis_description: credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] is a shared mapping across all arts for the same cred. When _initializePhiArt creates a new art for an existing credId, it overwrites the merkle root: credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] = merkleRootHash. This invalidates all pending merkle proofs for previously created arts sharing the same credId. Eligible users who had valid proofs against the old root can no longer claim.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a state corruption issue where creating a new art silently invalidates existing claim eligibility. A malicious or careless cred creator can weaponize this to selectively exclude users from minting.

proof_of_signal.pattern: credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] = merkleRootHash; — overwrites existing root for all arts sharing same credId

preconditions: Art A exists for credId X with MERKLE verification and merkle root R1
Art B is created for the same credId X with a different merkle root R2
Users have valid proofs against R1 but not R2

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] = merkleRootHash overwrites regardless of existing arts
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Cred creator creates new art with modified merkle root to exclude specific addresses
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Selective exclusion of eligible minters; denial of rightful NFT claims

economic_questions: How many arts share the same credId in practice?
Is there an on-chain mechanism to prevent merkle root overwrites?
Can affected users recover by obtaining new proofs against the new root?
validation_requirements: Create Art A with root R1, verify claims work, then create Art B with root R2, verify Art A claims fail
Determine if any guard prevents root overwrite
Assess frequency of multi-art per credId scenarios

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Art creation fee + valid signer signature
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Denial of NFT claims for affected users

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After users have obtained proofs for Art A but before they claim

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users with valid proofs against old merkle root
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All unclaimed mints for affected art

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Number of arts sharing credIds in production

threats.title: Missing Reentrancy Guard on Individual Share Trades Enables Cross-Function Reentrancy
threats.hypothesis_description: Individual buy/sell functions (buyShareCred, sellShareCred) call _handleTrade which lacks a nonReentrant modifier (only batch operations have it). During a sell, after share balance and supply are decremented, ETH is sent to the curator via safeTransferETH. If the curator is a contract, its receive() can reenter buyShareCred for the same credId at the now-reduced supply, obtaining shares at a cheaper price. While the attacker must hold through the lock period, they acquire shares at an artificial discount.
threats.investigation_rationale: The ETH transfer to the seller occurs after state updates but before protocol/creator fee transfers. A reentrant buy at reduced supply gets a lower price. The 10-minute lock prevents immediate resale but the discount persists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _handleTrade: state updated (supply decremented), then curator_.safeTransferETH(price - protocolFee - creatorFee) — external call before protocolFee and creatorFee transfers. No nonReentrant modifier.

preconditions: Attacker deploys a contract that acts as curator
Contract's receive() function calls buyShareCred
Attacker holds shares to sell, triggering the reentrant flow

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: After sell reduces supply by N, reentrant buy gets price at (supply-N) instead of supply
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Sell shares → receive ETH → reenter buy at reduced supply → acquire shares at discount

economic_questions: How much price discount is achievable at various supply levels?
Does the contract have sufficient ETH balance for protocol/creator fees after reentrancy?
Can the reentrancy cause the Cred contract to become insolvent?
validation_requirements: Deploy attacker contract with receive() that calls buyShareCred
Execute sell → reentrant buy sequence
Verify price discount and check if subsequent fee transfers succeed

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Bonding curve price difference at reduced supply
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on supply level and quantity traded

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Attacker must hold sufficient shares to sell
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: At higher supply levels where price curve is steeper
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could drain ETH from bonding curve reserve

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All cred share holders (bonding curve reserve depleted)
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced sell price if reserve is drained
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Bonding curve ETH reserve for affected cred

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Bonding curve price differences at various supply levels
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify reentrancy path and ETH balance sufficiency for fee transfers

threats.title: BondingCurve._getCreatorFee Missing Return Causes Incorrect Price Quotes at Supply Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: In BondingCurve._getCreatorFee, when supply_ == 0, creatorFee is set to 0 but there is no return statement. Execution falls through to calculate creatorFee = (price_ * royaltyRate) / RATIO_BASE, overwriting the 0. This means getBuyPriceAfterFee and getSellPriceAfterFee return incorrect (inflated) prices at supply=0, while the actual trade function getPriceData correctly returns creatorFee=0. Users see wrong prices.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the actual trade uses getPriceData (which is correct), the view functions getBuyPriceAfterFee/getSellPriceAfterFee are likely used by frontends and integrators for price display. The discrepancy between quoted and actual prices is a code defect that could mislead users.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _getCreatorFee: if (supply_ == 0) { creatorFee = 0; } — no return statement, execution continues to: creatorFee = (price_ * royaltyRate) / RATIO_BASE

preconditions: Cred supply is 0 (after all shares sold or before first share bought)
User queries getBuyPriceAfterFee or getSellPriceAfterFee

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _getCreatorFee sets creatorFee=0 for supply_==0 but lacks return; falls through to calculate non-zero fee
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Price quote shows higher price than actual trade cost at supply=0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Misleads users into avoiding purchases; potential for informed actors to buy at actual lower price

economic_questions: What is the price difference between quoted and actual at supply=0?
How often do creds reach supply=0?
Do any on-chain consumers use these view functions for trade decisions?
validation_requirements: Compare getBuyPriceAfterFee(credId, 0, 1) vs actual cost from getPriceData(credId, 0, 1, true)
Verify missing return statement in _getCreatorFee
Check if Cred.getCredBuyPriceWithFee uses getBuyPriceAfterFee (it does)

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Informed actors benefit from price discrepancy
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Up to royalty rate percentage of price at supply=0

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Inflated price quote causing overpayment estimates

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare getBuyPriceAfterFee vs getPriceData at supply=0

threats.title: Gas Limit DoS on CuratorRewardsDistributor.distribute() with Many Curators Permanently Locks Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: distribute() fetches ALL curator addresses via getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0) and iterates over them twice (once for share counting, once for reward calculation), then calls depositBatch with all addresses. For popular creds with hundreds of curators, this could exceed the block gas limit, permanently locking the accumulated reward balance with no recovery mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: An attacker can deliberately create many small share positions (1 share each from many addresses) to inflate the curator list. Once the list exceeds the gas limit threshold, rewards for that credId become permanently locked.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0) fetches unbounded array; two loops iterate over all curators; depositBatch sends to all

preconditions: Target credId has accumulated rewards in balanceOf[credId]
Number of unique curator addresses exceeds gas limit threshold for distribute()
No pagination or batch mechanism exists for distribute

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: O(N) iterations * 3 external calls per iteration (getCuratorAddresses loop, getShareNumber loop, depositBatch)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Buy 1 share from many addresses to inflate curator count beyond gas limit
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Permanently locks accumulated rewards; combined with other attacks could prevent legitimate distributions

economic_questions: What is the gas cost per curator in distribute()?
What is the maximum number of curators before exceeding block gas limit on Base/Optimism?
What is the cost to buy 1 share from N different addresses?
validation_requirements: Benchmark gas usage of distribute() with increasing curator counts
Calculate break-even: cost of share purchases vs locked reward value
Test distribute() at gas limit boundaries

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N * cost_of_1_share at various supply levels
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Not directly profitable; permanently locks victim rewards
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multiple transactions to build curator count, then permanent effect

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: ETH for many small share purchases
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Permanent reward lockup for targeted credId

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All curators of the targeted credId
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of accumulated curator rewards
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All current and future accumulated rewards for the credId

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Block gas limit, gas per iteration, curator count thresholds
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Gas profiling of distribute() with scaled curator lists

threats.title: handleRewardsAndGetValueSent Lacks Access Control Enabling Fake Reward Event Pollution
threats.hypothesis_description: PhiRewards.handleRewardsAndGetValueSent is external payable with NO access control. Anyone can call it directly (not just PhiNFT1155 contracts) by providing the required ETH. This deposits real rewards to arbitrary addresses and emits RewardsDeposit events that are indistinguishable from legitimate mint rewards, polluting the event log used for off-chain indexing and potentially gaming curator reward distributions via the curatorRewardsDistributor.deposit() call.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the caller must pay the actual ETH amount, they control the addresses (minter, receiver, referral, verifier) and the credId. This lets an attacker deposit curator rewards for specific credIds to manipulate distribution economics, or self-referral rewards by constructing artificial mint flows.

proof_of_signal.pattern: handleRewardsAndGetValueSent is external payable with no modifier restricting caller. Any address can call with arbitrary artId_, credId_, addressesData_

preconditions: Caller has enough ETH to cover computeMintReward(quantity_, mintFee_)
If chainSync_=true, curatorRewardsDistributor is set (non-zero)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No access control: any caller can deposit rewards to arbitrary credId via curatorRewardsDistributor.deposit
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call handleRewardsAndGetValueSent with chainSync_=true and target credId to inflate curator reward pool for credIds where attacker holds shares
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker deposits curate rewards to own credId, then calls distribute to reclaim disproportionate share

economic_questions: Can an attacker profitably cycle ETH through handleRewardsAndGetValueSent and distribute?
What fraction of the deposited ETH is recoverable through the curator reward path?
Are there losses due to protocol fees and rounding?
validation_requirements: Call handleRewardsAndGetValueSent directly with arbitrary parameters
Verify curatorRewardsDistributor receives deposit for specified credId
Calculate net ETH recovery after deposit → distribute → withdraw cycle

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Likely negative due to reward splits; useful for event pollution and targeted curator reward inflation
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Negative for pure ETH cycling; positive for event pollution goals

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When attacker holds dominant share position for target credId
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Corrupts off-chain indexing, inflates specific credId rewards

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Off-chain indexers; curators of manipulated credIds

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace ETH flow through handleRewardsAndGetValueSent with self-set addresses
